original_hypothesis.title: DelayBuffer.isUpdatable optimization can allow posting new delayed messages without applying valid delay proofs when buffer is synced+full, creating blind spots in delay tracking
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In delayProofImpl, buffer updates (and delay proof validation) are skipped if buffer.isUpdatable() is false (synced AND full). In that state, a batch poster can read new delayed messages without updating buffer reference points via proofs, potentially creating operational blind spots: monitoring and downstream assumptions that every read of new delayed messages updates buffer tracking may be violated, making it harder to attribute or detect delay anomalies around the synced window boundaries.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: May be intended by design, but it creates a gap where delayed message inclusion happens without processing the proof path. This is mostly an operational observability/integrity risk rather than direct theft.
deployment_assumptions: Operators/monitors assume delay proofs are processed whenever new delayed messages are read
Buffer frequently enters synced+full state due to configured threshold/max/replenishRate
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No deployment parameter values provided for buffer config; condition exists in SequencerInbox.delayProofImpl and DelayBuffer.isUpdatable.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (buffer.isUpdatable()) { ... validate proof ... buffer.update(...) } // else skip entirely

preconditions: buffer.isSynced() == true AND buffer.bufferBlocks == buffer.max (or buffer cannot be replenished)
Batch poster reads new delayed messages (afterDelayedMessagesRead > totalDelayedMessagesRead)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Proof path can be invoked but becomes a no-op depending on buffer state
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires batch poster role (trusted operator), not unprivileged attacker

operational_questions: Is skipping proof validation in synced+full state safe and intended for all monitoring assumptions?
Could buffer reference points drift in a way that later makes depletion calculations less representative?
Do nodes or off-chain systems rely on proof processing as an audit trail?
validation_requirements: Trace buffer.prevBlockNumber/prevSequencedBlockNumber evolution across batches with and without proof processing
Model whether skipping updates can alter future calcPendingBuffer outcomes at boundary transitions
Review operational monitoring rules for delay buffer health

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Post batches that read new delayed messages while providing delayProof but having it skipped
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Later, when buffer becomes updatable again, observe whether tracked delays differ from expected
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Use discrepancy to create monitoring confusion or dispute on delay behavior
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Low but requires batch poster cooperation/role

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Reduced accuracy/observability of delay tracking; potential incident-response ambiguity
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium; requires monitoring adjustments or code change
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Delay buffer observability and incident response

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Medium (depends on buffer state and operator behavior)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Batches reading delayed messages without corresponding buffer.update changes (needs state diffing)
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Delay buffer metrics not matching expected delayed queue behavior
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated delayed reads during long synced epochs

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
